macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek 3 and the Mishnah on Shomer's Payment Instead of Oath

micro_segments.title: Overview of Perek 3 structure and focus on special cases of shomrim liability
The Mishnah's case: shomer chinam paying instead of taking oath when object is stolen
The two scenarios for shomer's agreement to pay: admitting negligence or voluntary settlement

macro_segments.title: The Logic and Purpose of Keifel Payment to Shomer When Ganav is Found

micro_segments.title: Why does ganav pay keifel to shomer rather than to owner: takanah and owner's interests
Takanah as incentive for shomer to settle quickly and for court efficiency

macro_segments.title: Rava's Answer: Implicit Condition of Ownership Transfer in Case of Theft and Payment

micro_segments.title: Rava's mechanism: owner implicitly conditions that if stolen and shomer pays, ownership transfers to shomer retroactively
Rabbi Zeira's challenge: if shomer owns retroactively, shouldn't he own the shearings and offspring from the animal
Rabbi Zeira's answer: ownership transfer excludes shearings and offspring (gizos and velados)
Gemara's explanation: external benefits like keifel can be transferred, but benefits from the animal itself cannot

macro_segments.title: Alternative Answer: Ownership Transfer the Moment Before Theft (Samuch Ligenivas)

micro_segments.title: Alternative mechanism: shomer owns the moment before it gets stolen, not retroactively from transfer
Practical difference: this approach avoids Rabbi Zeira's challenge about shearings and offspring
Problem with moment-before answer: doesn't work when animal is stolen from a location not in shomer's possession (agam scenario)
Discussion of agam and Tosafos's alternative framing using retroactive ownership from present moment

macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Actual Payment (Shillem) and Promise to Pay (Ratza Leshalom)

micro_segments.title: Mishnah language 'he paid and did not want to swear' versus gemara's interpretation that promise suffices
Rabbi Yochanan: agreement to pay is enough; you don't need to actually pay (kiyan she'amar harei ani meshalem)

macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Actual Payment (Shillem) and Promise to Pay (Ratza Leshalom) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Analysis of Mishnah's structure: reisha and seifa are symmetrical but don't cover all cases
Gemara's ambiguity: unclear whether promise without payment gives keifel in all scenarios

macro_segments.title: Braita on Socher (Renter): Promise to Pay Suffices for Keifel

micro_segments.title: Braita: renter who agrees to pay rather than swear receives keifel when ganav is found
Discussion of socher's different legal position: legally obligated to pay in theft case, cannot swear falsely honestly
Proof from socher case: even when only promising (not paying), and even when already legally obligated, promise for keifel suffices

macro_segments.title: Cases of Changed Mind and Conditional Promises: Retraction and Timing Issues

micro_segments.title: Case: first says won't pay/will swear, then agrees to pay—obviously gets keifel despite delay
Case: agrees to pay then retracts—question whether he really can retract or just trying; either way, if retracted loses keifel
Gemara's view: either he can retract or he's bound but trying—either way, if retracted loses keifel; Tosafos: actually bound but attempting retraction loses keifel
Case: shomer dies after promising to pay but before paying; children refuse—question whether they're retracting or delaying
Case: shomer's children actually pay—question whether they get keifel given no gratitude from owner
Case: owner dies; shomer pays owner's heirs—question whether heirs' lack of prior relationship affects keifel eligibility
Case: both shomer and owner dead; heirs of shomer pay heirs of owner—question whether purely business consideration applies

macro_segments.title: Edge Cases: Partial Payment, Multiple Animals, Multiple Parties, Husband-Wife Scenarios

micro_segments.title: Case: shomer agrees to pay only half of principal—whether partial promise suffices for keifel
Case: shoal borrowed two cows, paid full for one—whether treated as separate transactions for keifel
Case: borrowed from partners, paid one partner's share—whether gets keifel for that half
Case: two partners borrowed, one pays his share—whether gets keifel for his portion
Case: woman borrows, husband pays on her behalf—question of owner identity and payment attribution
Gemara's conclusion on all partial/split cases: teiku (unresolved)

macro_segments.title: Expansion on Cases Receiving Keifel: Shomer Chinam, Shomer Sachar, and Shoal

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa: shomer chinam who admits negligence (pashati) gets keifel without even saying 'I will pay'
Shomer sachar who admits theft (nignava): gets keifel for honesty, incentivizing truth over false oath
Question of bifnim or chutz mipnim: whether these cases are within the property or outside
Shoal (borrower) exception: normally doesn't get keifel because has limited exemption options (only mita machmat melachah)
Reason for shoal exception: mita machmat melachah is rare, so no strong incentive needed to prevent false claim

macro_segments.title: Dispute: Whether Shoal Also Gets Keifel and the Basis for the Distinction

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa's second opinion: even shoal gets keifel when agrees to pay (different from his first opinion)
Rav Zevid reports Abaye's view: shoal gets keifel only if actually pays, not just promises
Abaye's reason: because shoal benefited from the loan and owner has no gratitude toward him
Tosafos: practical difference between approaches depends on whether shoal made special stipulations limiting his liability

macro_segments.title: Braita on Shoal: Actual Payment Required for Keifel

micro_segments.title: Braita: shoal who stepped forward and paid (hikdim v'shillem) gets keifel, emphasizing payment not promise
Contrast with socher braita: socher needs only to say (amar), shoal needs to step forward and pay (hikdim v'shillem)
Gemara's analysis: whether braitot are from same source and whether hikdim v'shillem means actual payment
Query to Tanaim of Bei Rabbi Chiyya and Bei Rabbi Oshaya: confirmation that braitot were taught together

macro_segments.title: Rav Huna's Requirement: Oath Even When Shomer Agrees to Pay (Mashbinan Shuva'ah)

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna: even if shomer agrees to pay, must still take oath that he's not holding object himself
Reason: concern that shomer might be falsely claiming loss to keep object for himself (chayishinan shema einav natan bah)
Impact: despite keifel incentive, court involvement and oath requirement still necessary to verify actual loss

topical_tags.term: Mita machmat melachah (death from normal use)
Rav Zevid / Rav Zvid (4th amora, Babylonia)
Einav natan bah (he set his eyes on it/fraudulent intent)
